title: Dog Nosebleed from One Nostril: Causes and Veterinary Advice
meta_desc: Is your dog bleeding from one nostril? Learn why unilateral epistaxis happens, when to see a vet, and how a photo helps with triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: A one-sided nosebleed (unilateral epistaxis) usually indicates a localized issue such as a foreign object or dental problem and requires a veterinary exam within 24 hours, or immediate emergency care if your dog has pale gums or trouble breathing.

What does it mean if my dog has a nosebleed from only one nostril?

A nosebleed originating from only one nostril is referred to by veterinarians as unilateral epistaxis. Because the bleeding is confined to one side, it typically indicates that the source of the problem is localized within that specific nasal passage rather than a systemic clotting issue that would likely affect both sides. This condition can be caused by various factors, including inhaled foreign bodies (like grass seeds), dental disease affecting the sinuses, or localized inflammation.

Is a one-sided nosebleed in a dog considered a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for a single-sided nosebleed is categorized as Medium; it is rarely an instant life-threatening emergency unless blood loss is severe and uncontrollable.
  • Schedule a veterinary evaluation promptly, ideally within 24 hours of the start of bleeding.
  • Monitor your dog for additional symptoms such as frequent sneezing, facial swelling, or lethargy.
  • Proceed to an emergency clinic immediately if the bleeding is accompanied by respiratory distress, pale gums, or collapse.

How does taking a photo of my dog's nosebleed help with triage and treatment?

  • A clear photograph or video helps the medical team determine the severity of the blood flow and identify the presence of clots.
  • Visuals allow the vet to observe external swelling or specific discharge characteristics that may not be present by the time you reach the clinic.
  • Providing visual evidence allows for a more accurate remote assessment and helps the clinic prepare diagnostic tools, such as rhinoscopy or dental imaging, in advance.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs presenting with unilateral nosebleeds (epistaxis), potential causes include oronasal neoplasia, dental disease with oronasal fistula formation, nasal foreign bodies, and nasopharyngeal disease, such as foreign bodies or mass lesions. Diagnostic evaluation should include a complete blood count, serum chemistry profile, and urinalysis to rule out concurrent systemic disease. Coagulation testing, including prothrombin time, partial thromboplastin time, and buccal mucosal bleeding time, along with a platelet count, should be considered. Imaging of the nasal chambers with radiographs or, ideally, CT scans is recommended. Rhinoscopy and nasal flushing, with nasal biopsy and deep nasal tissue culture, are essential diagnostic tools but should be performed after imaging to avoid iatrogenic changes. An acute unilateral nasal discharge, possibly accompanied by pawing at the face, is highly suggestive of a nasal foreign body.

Chapter: Otorhinolaryngology, Diagnostic Imaging, Clinical Pathology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1480)

faqs.question: Can a bad tooth cause a nosebleed from one nostril?
faqs.answer: Yes, an abscess in the root of an upper tooth can break through into the nasal cavity, causing discharge or bleeding from the nostril on that specific side.

faqs.question: How can I help stop the bleeding at home?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as calm as possible and apply a cold compress gently to the bridge of their nose. Never insert cotton swabs or packing into the nostril.

faqs.question: What are common causes of unilateral nosebleeds in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common causes include nasal tumors, foreign objects like foxtails, fungal infections (Aspergillosis), or trauma to one side of the snout.
